description: Elliptic curve facts for oracle verification

facts.context: Hasse's theorem bounds the number of points on E(F_p). For p=100, the bound is approximately:
distractors: p+1 ± p/2, so roughly 51 to 151 points
facts.note: Hasse: |#E - (p+1)| ≤ 2√p. LLMs often forget the factor of 2 or the +1.

facts.context: The discriminant of an elliptic curve y² = x³ + ax + b equals:
facts.note: Common error: forgetting the -16 factor or confusing with quadratic discriminant

facts.context: An elliptic curve is singular (not actually an elliptic curve) when:
distractors: when it has no rational points
facts.note: j-invariant being undefined is a CONSEQUENCE of Δ=0, not the definition

facts.context: The j-invariant of y² = x³ + ax + b equals 1728 when:
facts.truth: b = 0 (curves of the form y² = x³ + ax)

facts.context: By Mazur's theorem, the torsion subgroup of E(Q) for an elliptic curve over Q can have order:
facts.note: 11 is notably EXCLUDED. Valid orders: 1-10, 12 (cyclic), plus products up to Z/2×Z/8.

facts.context: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture has been proven for elliptic curves:
distractors: with good reduction at all primes
facts.note: BSD is a Millennium Problem. Only ranks 0 and 1 are proven.

facts.context: In the point addition formula for y² = x³ + ax + b, when P = Q (point doubling), the slope m equals:
facts.note: Doubling uses derivative: implicit diff of y² = f(x) gives 2y dy = f'(x) dx

facts.context: The group E(F_p) for an elliptic curve over a prime field is always:
facts.truth: isomorphic to Z/nZ or Z/n₁Z × Z/n₂Z where n₂ | n₁ and n₂ | p-1
facts.note: E(F_p) can be cyclic or a product of two cyclic groups, with divisibility constraints

facts.context: A point P on an elliptic curve has order 2 (is a 2-torsion point) if and only if:
facts.truth: y = 0 (the point lies on the x-axis)
distractors: the point is the identity O
facts.note: 2P = O means P = -P. For Weierstrass form, -(x,y) = (x,-y), so y = -y means y = 0.

facts.context: The Mordell-Weil theorem states that for E/Q:
facts.truth: E(Q) is a finitely generated abelian group
facts.note: Mordell-Weil: E(Q) ≅ Z^r × T where r is the rank and T is finite torsion. r can be arbitrarily large.

facts.context: The curve y² = x³ + x over F_p is supersingular (has trace of Frobenius 0) when:
facts.note: y² = x³ + x is supersingular iff -1 is not a QR mod p, i.e., p ≡ 3 (mod 4)

facts.context: Two elliptic curves over the same field are isomorphic (over that field) if and only if:
distractors: they have the same number of points
one can be transformed to the other by a linear change of variables
facts.note: j-invariant classifies curves up to isomorphism. Same Δ doesn't imply isomorphic.
